metadata.title: The Chinese Room.
metadata.greeting_note: Māori greeting — Liam persona. Never Wagwan (Bear's alone).
metadata.thesis: Simulation is not understanding. And that's the sharpest question in AI.
metadata.note: Vox mixed-media reel converted from chinese-room-explainer (NEU brand). LIAM voice — Kokoro am_onyx. IN-FOR-BEAR LAW applied: B00 narration opens 'this is Liam, in for Bear', B07 outro signs off 'Liam, in for Bear'. Source ink illustrations preserved as STILL/kenburns beats. Remotion patterns cover B00 (opener), B02 (room concept), B06 (verdict), B07 (outro). B01/B03/B04/B05 are ink-illustration STILLs from public/chinese-room-explainer/.

beats.narration_text: Can a machine ever truly think? Not just respond — but understand? This is Liam, in for Bear. One philosopher built an answer inside a room — and it's been rattling around in AI ever since.

output: simulation is not the same as understanding
thinking might be behaviour, not inner state
Searle vs Turing — one room, two positions

beats.narration_text: That philosopher: John Searle. Philosopher. Rhodes scholar. In 1980 he set out to dismantle the entire premise of strong AI — using nothing but a thought experiment.

shot.asset_origin: public/chinese-room-explainer/searle_1.png — editorial ink portrait, Searle at desk with book

beats.narration_text: Picture a sealed room. You're inside. All around you: crates of Chinese characters — symbols you cannot read. One book, written in English, with rules for matching them.

props.heading: picture this
props.body: You're inside. All around you: crates of Chinese characters — symbols you cannot read. One book, written in English. Full of rules for matching them.

beats.narration_text: A message slides under the door. You don't understand a word. But the book tells you exactly which symbols to send back. You do. The reply slips out.

shot.asset_origin: public/chinese-room-explainer/searle_03.png — ink illustration, Searle surrounded by papers and symbols (the shuffling paper image)

beats.narration_text: From outside, it looks like fluent conversation. The person out there is certain they're speaking with someone who knows Chinese. They're not. They're speaking with a man shuffling paper.

shot.asset_origin: public/chinese-room-explainer/chinese-speaker-04.png — ink illustration, person holding a message/letter (the outside perspective)

beats.narration_text: Alan Turing saw it differently. Thinking is not a hidden inner state — it's a behaviour. Converse convincingly enough, and you think. That's the Turing Test.

Searle's answer: simulation is not understanding. A program that passes the test doesn't grasp meaning — it shuffles symbols. Though, if we're being honest — most human conversations don't clear that bar either.

artifactLines: Simulation is not understanding: passing the Turing Test does not mean you grasp meaning.
The mechanism: a man in a room shuffles Chinese symbols by rule. Fluent output, zero comprehension.
Turing's counter: thinking IS behaviour — converse convincingly enough, and you think.
Searle's hold: behaviour without meaning is not thought. The room understands nothing.
The punchline: most human conversations don't clear that bar either.

beats.narration_text: The Chinese Room. Liam, in for Bear.
